title: Psalms 86
subtitle: A Prayer of David: Hear Me and Answer Me

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — “You, Lord, Are Good and Forgiving”

heb.paragraph: "Guard my life, for I am faithful (ḥāsîd); you are my God; save your servant who trusts in you" (v.2). The only psalm attributed to David in Book III. Multiple petitions: hear, have mercy, guard, save, bring joy. "You, Lord, are forgiving and good, abounding in love to all who call to you" (v.5). The longest list of divine attributes in a single psalm.

notes.note: "Hear me, for I am poor and needy. Guard my life, for I am faithful." Two self-descriptions: poor/needy (condition) and faithful (character). Both argue for God’s attention.
"You, Lord, are forgiving and good, abounding in love to all who call to you." The character of God as the basis for prayer.

refs.note: The psalm quotes the divine self-revelation: 'compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love.'
Jonah cites the same formula when complaining about God's mercy to Nineveh.
The Lord's compassion and mercy as demonstrated to Job echoes this psalm's confidence.

hist.context: Psalm 86 is the only Davidic psalm in Book III (Pss 73-89). It weaves together phrases from other psalms and Torah texts, functioning almost as a mosaic of earlier prayers. The self-description 'poor and needy' (v.1) identifies with the vulnerable class frequently mentioned in psalms. The 'undivided heart' request (v.11) seeks single-minded devotion.

sections.header: Verses 10–17 — “Teach Me Your Way, LORD, That I May Rely on Your Faithfulness”

heb.paragraph: "Give me an undivided heart (yaḥēd ləbābî), that I may fear your name" (v.11). The request for internal unity: a single heart, not split between God and the world. "You are great and do marvellous deeds; you alone are God" (v.10). Among the gods there is none like you (v.8). "Teach me your way, LORD, that I may rely on your faithfulness" (v.11).

notes.note: "For you are great and do marvellous deeds; you alone are God." The incomparability declaration. No rival, no equal, no comparison.
"Teach me your way, LORD, that I may rely on your faithfulness; give me an undivided heart." The undivided heart: the opposite of Psalm 12’s "double heart." Integrity = singularity of devotion.

"You, Lord, are a compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness." Echoes Exodus 34:6 — the divine self-description at Sinai. This is who God revealed himself to be.

refs.note: All nations worshiping before God because his righteous acts are revealed fulfills the psalm's vision.
Every knee bowing and tongue confessing develops the psalm's universal worship theme.
Every knee bowing to YHWH parallels the psalm's prediction that all nations will worship.

hist.context: The confession 'you alone are God' (v.10) is unambiguously monotheistic, distinguishing YHWH from all supposed deities. The closing plea for a 'sign of your goodness' (v.17) requests visible evidence that God is on the psalmist's side, silencing enemies. Such signs might include deliverance, restored health, or public vindication.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in A Prayer of David: Hear Me and Answer Me.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The character of God as the basis for prayer…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou, Lord, are forgiving and good, abounding in love to all who call to you.ESVFor you, O Lord, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love to all who call upon you.NIVBring joy to your servant, Lord, for I put my trust in you.ESVGladden the soul of your servant, for to you, O Lord, do I lift up my soul.

rec.text: ""Hear me, for I am poor and needy. Guard my life, for I am faithful." Two self-descriptions: poor/needy (condition) and faithful (character). Both argue for God’s attention."

rows.label: Forgiving and Good — 86:1–9
rows.text: "You are forgiving and good, abounding in love." Poor, needy, faithful. Longest attribute list.

hebtext.note: "Guard my life, for I am faithful (ḥāsîd); you are my God; save your servant who trusts in you" (v.2). The only psalm attributed to David in Book III. Multiple petitions: hear, have mercy, guard, save, bring joy. "You, Lord, are forgiving and good, abounding in love to all who call to you" (v.5). The longest list of divine attributes in a single psalm.

"Give me an undivided heart (yaḥēd ləbābî), that I may fear your name" (v.11). The request for internal unity: a single heart, not split between God and the world. "You are great and do marvellous deeds; you alone are God" (v.10). Among the gods there is none like you (v.8). "Teach me your way, LORD, that I may rely on your faithfulness" (v.11).

themes.note: Mercy peaks: the character of God as prayer’s foundation. Faith: the undivided heart. The Exodus 34:6 echo.

prayer_prompt: David pleads for God's attention in the midst of trouble. Consider asking God about the troubles that feel overwhelming right now — and whether you've brought them to Him with this kind of directness.
